item.title: What Happens When a Woman Lives With 32 Men?!
item.description: In 1944, Kazuko Higa was living on Anatahan Island in the Northern Mariana Islands when 31 Japanese shipwreck survivors swam ashore, leaving her the only woman among 32 men. Eleven men died under unresolved circumstances over the following years. The group refused to believe the war had ended and remained on the island until 1951. Kazuko escaped after learning the men planned to harm her and was rescued by a passing American vessel. The 19 surviving men surrendered shortly after. She returned to Japan briefly as a public figure before fading from view and died in 1971.

media:content.media:title: What Happens When a Woman Lives With 32 Men?!
media:content.media:description: In 1944, Kazuko Higa was living on Anatahan Island in the Northern Mariana Islands when 31 Japanese shipwreck survivors swam ashore, leaving her the only woman among 32 men. Eleven men died under unresolved circumstances over the following years. The group refused to believe the war had ended and remained on the island until 1951. Kazuko escaped after learning the men planned to harm her and was rescued by a passing American vessel. The 19 surviving men surrendered shortly after. She returned to Japan briefly as a public figure before fading from view and died in 1971.

item.title: He Pretended to Be a Woman and Tricked Over 1,600 Men
item.description: In July 2025, a man in Nanjing known online as “Sister Hong” was arrested after posing as a woman to arrange encounters with men, which were secretly recorded and shared online. Authorities said the content was distributed for profit, triggering a criminal investigation. Initial online claims suggested over 1,600 victims, but officials later stated that number was exaggerated, with the true total not publicly confirmed. The case raised serious concerns about privacy violations and consent. The incident quickly went viral across social media, generating widespread discussion and debate. Despite the severity of the allegations, the case also became the subject of memes and online parody, drawing criticism over how it was being treated publicly.

media:content.media:title: He Pretended to Be a Woman and Tricked Over 1,600 Men
media:content.media:description: In July 2025, a man in Nanjing known online as “Sister Hong” was arrested after posing as a woman to arrange encounters with men, which were secretly recorded and shared online. Authorities said the content was distributed for profit, triggering a criminal investigation. Initial online claims suggested over 1,600 victims, but officials later stated that number was exaggerated, with the true total not publicly confirmed. The case raised serious concerns about privacy violations and consent. The incident quickly went viral across social media, generating widespread discussion and debate. Despite the severity of the allegations, the case also became the subject of memes and online parody, drawing criticism over how it was being treated publicly.
